report_metadata.cancer_type: Nodal Marginal Zone Lymphoma (NMZL) of the Neck

treatments.name: Involved-Site Radiation Therapy (ISRT) 24-30 Gy

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN Category 2A recommendation for localized (Stage I-II) nodal MZL. Extensive retrospective data with consistent results across multiple institutions. ILROG guidelines standardize the approach. 5-year DFS 74%, OS 97% in localized MZL.

survival_benefit.rationale: Exceptional outcomes for localized disease: 5-year OS 97%, 5-year DFS 74%, CR rate 99% (87/88 patients). In-field recurrence rate extremely low at 2.4% at 5 years and 4.7% at 10 years. For Stage II bilateral cervical nodes, curative intent is realistic.

accessibility.rationale: Widely available at all radiation oncology centers. Standard of care for localized MZL per NCCN, ESMO, and BSH guidelines. No special equipment beyond standard linear accelerator required.

safety_profile.rationale: Low-dose ISRT (24 Gy) is well-tolerated with minimal acute toxicity. Cervical radiation may cause temporary mucositis, xerostomia (concerning given existing Sjogren's dryness), and fatigue. Long-term risks include secondary malignancy but are low at these doses.

biomarker_match.rationale: Radiation is effective regardless of biomarker profile. t(11;18) negative status is associated with better local control. Localized bilateral cervical disease is amenable to involved-site technique. Stage II is within curative RT range.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Ionizing radiation induces DNA double-strand breaks in lymphoma cells, leading to apoptosis and cell death. ISRT targets the involved nodal regions with a margin for microscopic disease while minimizing exposure to uninvolved tissue. Low-dose (24 Gy) is sufficient for indolent lymphomas due to their high radiosensitivity.

key_evidence.study_name: ILROG ISRT Guidelines / Multi-institutional retrospective series
key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology, Biology, Physics

biomarker_requirements: None required - effective regardless of molecular profile
notable_side_effects: Acute: mild mucositis, xerostomia (caution with Sjogren's), fatigue, skin erythema
Late: hypothyroidism (if thyroid in field), secondary malignancy risk (very low at 24 Gy)
Worsening of Sjogren's-related dry mouth possible

treatments.name: Rituximab Monotherapy (375 mg/m2 weekly x4 or extended)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 and large retrospective studies support rituximab monotherapy in nodal MZL. Dual-institution study showed excellent long-term outcomes in rituximab era with median PFS 42.4 months and 10-year OS 71.9%. NCCN lists as first-line option for MZL.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS not reached in multiple studies. 10-year OS approximately 72%. Survival not significantly different from multi-agent chemoimmunotherapy in nodal MZL. Additionally treats Sjogren's syndrome B-cell activation, providing dual benefit.

accessibility.rationale: Rituximab is FDA-approved, widely available, and generic/biosimilar versions available. CD20+ status confirmed, making patient eligible. Standard outpatient infusion; no hospitalization required.

safety_profile.rationale: Very well-tolerated monotherapy. Main risks are infusion reactions (manageable with premedication), hypogammaglobulinemia, and minor infection risk. Avoids chemotherapy toxicity entirely. Favorable for younger patients wanting to preserve fertility and quality of life.

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20+ confirmed - rituximab directly targets the CD20 antigen on lymphoma cells. Dual mechanism: depletes malignant B cells and Sjogren's-associated autoreactive B cells. Ideal biomarker match for this patient.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Rituximab is a chimeric anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that targets the CD20 surface antigen on B cells, inducing cell death through complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC), antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), and direct apoptosis. In Sjogren's-associated MZL, rituximab additionally depletes autoreactive B cells driving the autoimmune process.

key_evidence.study_name: Dual institution experience of nodal marginal zone lymphoma in the rituximab era

notable_side_effects: Increased infection risk (hepatitis B reactivation screening required)

treatments.name: Active Surveillance (Watch-and-Wait)

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN guidelines endorse active surveillance for asymptomatic indolent NHL including MZL regardless of stage. Long-established approach with decades of evidence showing no OS detriment from deferring treatment in asymptomatic patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: No direct survival benefit but also no detriment compared to immediate treatment in asymptomatic patients. Avoids treatment toxicity. Median time to treatment ~58 months. However, for Sjogren's-associated MZL, systemic treatment may reduce autoimmune flares.

accessibility.rationale: No treatment required - only regular clinical monitoring with physical exam, labs (CBC, LDH, beta-2 microglobulin), and imaging every 3-6 months. Universally available and no cost for drugs.

safety_profile.rationale: No treatment toxicity whatsoever. Preserves quality of life and avoids immunosuppression. Psychological burden of untreated cancer is the main adverse effect. Allows for natural immune surveillance.

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to all MZL regardless of biomarkers. However, Sjogren's association argues for systemic treatment to address underlying autoimmune drive. Stage II bilateral disease may also warrant treatment consideration over observation.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Active surveillance involves regular clinical monitoring without therapeutic intervention, based on the principle that indolent lymphomas may remain stable or progress very slowly over years. Treatment is initiated only upon development of symptoms, rapid progression, organ compromise, cytopenias, or patient preference. Monitoring includes physical exams, laboratory tests, and periodic imaging.

key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology / NCCN Guidelines

biomarker_requirements: None - applicable to all patients with indolent MZL
notable_side_effects: Psychological distress from living with untreated cancer
Risk of disease progression requiring eventual treatment
Risk of histologic transformation (approximately 3-5% over 10 years)
Ongoing Sjogren's flares without systemic B-cell depletion

treatments.name: Rituximab + ISRT Combined Modality Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN lists combined modality therapy (immunotherapy + RT) as an option for Stage II MZL. Retrospective studies support the combination. No randomized Phase 3 comparing combined modality vs single modality in MZL Stage II.

survival_benefit.rationale: Combines systemic disease control (rituximab) with definitive local therapy (RT). May reduce distant relapse risk beyond RT alone. Rituximab addresses systemic Sjogren's-driven B-cell proliferation while RT eradicates bulky cervical disease.

accessibility.rationale: Both rituximab and radiation therapy are widely available, FDA-approved, and standard treatments. Can be administered at most oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined toxicity of both modalities but generally well tolerated. Rituximab infusion reactions plus RT-related mucositis and xerostomia. Additive immunosuppression modest. Sjogren's dry mouth may be exacerbated by cervical RT.

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20+ confirmed for rituximab. Bilateral cervical stage II disease is ideal for ISRT. Combined approach addresses both local disease and systemic Sjogren's-related B-cell dysregulation.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combined modality therapy pairs systemic anti-CD20 immunotherapy with localized radiation to achieve both systemic B-cell depletion and definitive local tumor control. Rituximab eradicates circulating and distant CD20+ cells while ISRT provides high local cure rates for involved cervical nodes. The combination may be synergistic through radiation-induced immunogenic cell death enhancing anti-CD20 effects.

key_evidence.journal: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology

notable_side_effects: Combined infusion reactions and radiation side effects

treatments.name: Bendamustine plus Rituximab (BR)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 StiL NHL1 and BRIGHT trials established BR as standard first-line for indolent NHL. PFS 69.5 months (BR) vs 31.2 months (R-CHOP). However, for MZL specifically, PFS advantage was not significant vs R-CHOP, suggesting comparable efficacy.

survival_benefit.rationale: In MZL subgroup of StiL NHL1, PFS was comparable to R-CHOP. 5-year OS ~80%. For a 45-year-old with Stage II disease, BR may be more than needed given excellent RT and rituximab monotherapy responses. Reserved for symptomatic or advanced disease.

accessibility.rationale: Both bendamustine and rituximab are FDA-approved and widely available. Standard outpatient chemotherapy regimen administered at most oncology centers. Generics available.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than R-CHOP with less alopecia and neuropathy, but significant hematologic toxicity including prolonged lymphopenia, neutropenia, and infection risk. More immunosuppressive than rituximab alone, relevant for Sjogren's patient.

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20+ confirmed for rituximab component. Bendamustine is a bifunctional alkylator effective in B-cell lymphomas regardless of specific molecular features. BCL2+ does not limit efficacy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Bendamustine is a bifunctional alkylating agent with purine analog properties that induces DNA crosslinks, single and double strand breaks, and apoptosis via multiple pathways including p53-dependent and mitotic catastrophe mechanisms. Combined with rituximab's anti-CD20 targeting, BR provides synergistic cytotoxicity against B-cell lymphomas.
notable_side_effects: Skin reactions including erythema and rash

treatments.name: R-CVP (Rituximab, Cyclophosphamide, Vincristine, Prednisone)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 PLRG4 trial compared R-CVP vs R-CHOP in indolent NHL. NCCN preferred first-line regimen for MZL. 5-year PFS 69%, 5-year OS 89% with R-CVP. Well-established regimen with decades of evidence in indolent lymphomas.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 89% (comparable to R-CHOP 84%). 5-year PFS 69%. ORR ~97% with CR rate ~37%. For a 45-year-old with Stage II disease, may be more aggressive than needed given excellent RT/rituximab monotherapy options.

accessibility.rationale: All components are generic and widely available. Standard chemotherapy regimen administered at all oncology centers. Low cost. No special biomarker testing required.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than R-CHOP with less neutropenia (3.4% vs 11.6%), fewer infections (2.5% vs 10.7%), and no anthracycline cardiac toxicity. Grade III/IV AEs in 15.3%. No neuropathy risk from vincristine is modest.

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20+ for rituximab component. Chemotherapy backbone effective regardless of specific molecular features. BCL2+ status does not limit efficacy. Broadly applicable across MZL subtypes.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: R-CVP combines anti-CD20 immunotherapy (rituximab) with alkylating agent cyclophosphamide, vinca alkaloid vincristine, and corticosteroid prednisone. Cyclophosphamide crosslinks DNA causing cell death, vincristine disrupts microtubule formation blocking mitosis, and prednisone induces lymphocyte apoptosis and reduces inflammation. The multi-agent approach targets lymphoma cells through complementary mechanisms.
notable_side_effects: Steroid side effects (insomnia, mood changes, hyperglycemia)

treatments.name: Zanubrutinib (Brukinsa) Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: MAGNOLIA Phase 2 trial with 68 patients showed IRC-assessed ORR 68.2%, CR 25.8%. FDA accelerated approval Sept 2021 for R/R MZL after ≥1 anti-CD20 regimen. 24-month DOR event-free rate 72.9%. Nodal MZL subgroup ORR 76%.

survival_benefit.rationale: 24-month PFS rate 70.9%, 24-month OS rate 85.9%. Durable disease control demonstrated. Median time to response 2.8 months. Continuous oral therapy maintains disease suppression. Superior to ibrutinib in safety profile.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved under accelerated approval for R/R MZL after ≥1 anti-CD20 regimen. Oral once-daily dosing. Available as specialty pharmacy prescription. Second-generation BTK inhibitor with better availability than ibrutinib (which lost MZL indication).

safety_profile.rationale: Favorable safety vs first-generation BTK inhibitors. Low atrial fibrillation rate (2.9%), low hypertension (4.4%). Grade ≥3 neutropenia 11.8%. Discontinuation for AEs only 7.4%. Common AEs: contusion (23.5%), diarrhea (22.1%), constipation (17.6%).

biomarker_match.rationale: BTK is constitutively active in MZL cells. Effective across MZL subtypes with highest ORR in nodal MZL (76%). No specific biomarker required beyond MZL diagnosis. Works in CD20+ and CD20- disease.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Zanubrutinib is a highly selective, next-generation covalent Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor that blocks B-cell receptor signaling essential for MZL cell survival and proliferation. By selectively inhibiting BTK with less off-target kinase inhibition than ibrutinib, zanubrutinib achieves effective lymphoma control with reduced cardiac and bleeding toxicity.
biomarker_requirements: Prior anti-CD20 regimen required for FDA-approved indication
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (accelerated approval for R/R MZL after ≥1 anti-CD20 regimen)

treatments.name: Lenalidomide + Rituximab (R2)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 MAGNIFY trial and AUGMENT trial support R2 in MZL. FDA-approved for MZL after at least one prior therapy. ORR 66% with CR 44% in MZL in MAGNIFY. Nodal MZL subgroup showed 57% ORR with 57% CR rate.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median DOR not reached at 11.5 months follow-up. Durable responses demonstrated. PFS improvement over rituximab alone in relapsed indolent NHL (AUGMENT). Chemo-free regimen with sustained disease control.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for relapsed/refractory MZL. Lenalidomide requires REMS program enrollment. Oral dosing is convenient. Available at most oncology centers. Cost may be a factor but generally covered by insurance.

safety_profile.rationale: Notable toxicities include neutropenia (43% any grade, 37% grade 3/4), fatigue (47%), diarrhea (37%), nausea (30%), constipation (30%). Thrombocytopenia risk. Teratogenic - requires REMS. Tumor flare reaction possible.

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20+ for rituximab component. Lenalidomide is immunomodulatory and works regardless of specific genetic markers. t(11;18) negative status does not affect efficacy. Effective across MZL subtypes.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lenalidomide is an immunomodulatory agent that enhances NK cell and T-cell mediated anti-tumor immunity, inhibits angiogenesis, and directly induces apoptosis in malignant B cells through cereblon-mediated degradation of IKZF1/IKZF3 transcription factors. Combined with rituximab, lenalidomide augments ADCC and complement-dependent cytotoxicity, creating synergistic anti-lymphoma activity.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (for R/R MZL after ≥1 prior therapy)

treatments.name: R-CHOP (Rituximab, Cyclophosphamide, Doxorubicin, Vincristine, Prednisone)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 data from StiL NHL1, BRIGHT, and PLRG4 trials. NCCN preferred first-line for aggressive or symptomatic indolent NHL. 5-year OS 84%, 5-year PFS 71% in R-CHOP arm of PLRG4. Established gold standard for decades.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 84%, 5-year PFS 71%. CR rate 43.6% in PLRG4. In MZL, survival outcomes not significantly different from rituximab monotherapy. Over-treatment concern for Stage II indolent MZL in young patient.

accessibility.rationale: All components generic and universally available. Standard regimen at all oncology centers worldwide. No special biomarker testing or authorization required.

safety_profile.rationale: Most toxic of first-line options: grade III/IV AEs in 33.1%, neutropenia 11.6%, infections 10.7%. Anthracycline (doxorubicin) cardiac toxicity cumulative and irreversible. Alopecia, nausea, neuropathy. Disproportionate toxicity for indolent disease.

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20+ for rituximab component. Multi-agent chemotherapy effective regardless of molecular features. BCL2+ does not limit efficacy. However, for indolent MZL, the aggressive regimen is generally not preferred.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: R-CHOP combines rituximab (anti-CD20) with cyclophosphamide (alkylating agent), doxorubicin (anthracycline topoisomerase II inhibitor causing DNA intercalation), vincristine (microtubule disruptor), and prednisone (corticosteroid). This multi-agent approach targets rapidly dividing cells through multiple mechanisms while rituximab provides targeted CD20-directed immunotherapy.

key_evidence.journal: British Journal of Haematology / The Lancet

treatments.name: Mosunetuzumab (Lunsumio) - CD20xCD3 Bispecific Antibody

evidence_level.rationale: FDA-approved for R/R follicular lymphoma. Phase 1/2 data in indolent NHL including MZL shows ORR 65.7% with median DOR 23.2 months. Phase 2 study with lenalidomide augmentation shows CR 88% in MZL. Not yet specifically approved for MZL.

survival_benefit.rationale: High CR rate (88%) in untreated MZL when combined with lenalidomide augmentation. 12-month PFS 87%. Fixed-duration treatment (unlike continuous BTK inhibitors). Durable responses in complete responders with median DOR not reached.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for follicular lymphoma but used off-label for MZL. Available at major cancer centers. Step-up dosing required for CRS mitigation. Outpatient subcutaneous administration is convenient.

safety_profile.rationale: CRS is the main concern but manageable with step-up dosing. Fixed-duration treatment limits prolonged immunosuppression. Off-the-shelf (no manufacturing delay like CAR-T). Hypogammaglobulinemia and infection risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets CD20 (confirmed positive in this patient) and redirects CD3+ T cells to kill lymphoma cells. Highly specific mechanism. May be effective even in rituximab-refractory disease. Complements the CD20+ biomarker profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Mosunetuzumab is a CD20-directed CD3 T-cell engaging bispecific antibody that simultaneously binds CD20 on lymphoma cells and CD3 on T cells, forming an immunological synapse that redirects endogenous T cells to kill CD20-expressing malignant B cells. This mechanism bypasses the need for traditional immune activation and is effective even in patients with impaired immune function.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 2 mosunetuzumab with response-driven lenalidomide augmentation

notable_side_effects: Tumor lysis syndrome (rare in indolent NHL)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for R/R FL; Off-label for MZL / Clinical Trials

treatments.name: Rituximab Maintenance (post-induction)

evidence_level.rationale: StiL NHL7-2008 MAINTAIN trial evaluated rituximab maintenance after BR in indolent NHL. Real-world evidence from 10 US centers. Benefit unclear in MZL specifically - no significant PFS or OS difference with maintenance vs observation in MZL.

survival_benefit.rationale: No statistically significant PFS or OS improvement with rituximab maintenance in MZL specifically. May benefit certain high-risk subgroups. Prolonged B-cell depletion may help control Sjogren's activity but adds immunosuppression risk.

accessibility.rationale: Rituximab widely available and affordable (biosimilars). Standard outpatient infusion every 2-3 months. Well-established infrastructure. Covered by insurance as standard lymphoma management.

safety_profile.rationale: Prolonged hypogammaglobulinemia and infection risk with extended B-cell depletion. Infusion reactions less frequent with subsequent doses. Late-onset neutropenia possible. May impair vaccination response.

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20+ confirmed. Maintenance rituximab keeps CD20+ B cells suppressed. For Sjogren's-associated MZL, prolonged B-cell depletion may reduce autoimmune flares and potentially lower lymphoma relapse risk.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Rituximab maintenance therapy involves periodic anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody infusions (typically every 8-12 weeks for 2 years) after initial induction therapy to maintain B-cell depletion, prevent lymphoma regrowth, and sustain remission. In Sjogren's-associated MZL, maintenance B-cell depletion may additionally suppress the autoimmune process driving lymphomagenesis.

key_evidence.study_name: StiL NHL7-2008 MAINTAIN / Real-world evidence from 10 US centers

treatments.name: Lisocabtagene Maraleucel (Breyanzi) - CAR-T Cell Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: FDA-approved Dec 2025 for R/R MZL after ≥2 prior lines. TRANSCEND FL-MZL trial showed ORR 84.4% (ITT) and 95.5% (efficacy evaluable), CR 55.8%. First CAR-T approved for MZL. Phase 2 single-arm study.

survival_benefit.rationale: Exceptional response rates: ORR 95.5%, CR 55.8% in heavily pretreated patients. Median DOR not reached. 24-month PFS 85.7%, OS 90.4%. Potentially curative in relapsed/refractory setting.

accessibility.rationale: Only available for R/R MZL after ≥2 prior lines (not applicable to this patient's first-line setting). Requires specialized CAR-T centers, leukapheresis, manufacturing time. Extremely high cost (~$400,000+). Limited to major academic centers.

safety_profile.rationale: CRS in 76% (grade ≥3 in 4.5%). Neurologic toxicity risk. Prolonged cytopenias, hypogammaglobulinemia, infection risk. Requires hospitalization for monitoring. HLH-like syndrome possible. However, CRS rates manageable with tocilizumab.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets CD19 which is expressed on MZL cells. CD20+ and CD19+ coexpression is typical in MZL. First and only CAR-T specifically approved for MZL. Applicable regardless of t(11;18) or BCL2 status.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lisocabtagene maraleucel is an autologous anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy consisting of patient-derived T cells genetically modified to express a chimeric antigen receptor targeting CD19. The defined 1:1 ratio of CD4 and CD8 CAR-T cells enables potent and sustained killing of CD19-expressing lymphoma cells through direct cytotoxicity, cytokine release, and immunological memory formation.
biomarker_requirements: Requires ≥2 prior lines of systemic therapy
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (Dec 2025, for R/R MZL after ≥2 prior systemic therapies)

treatments.name: Sjogren's Syndrome Disease-Directed Therapy (Hydroxychloroquine + Immunomodulation)

evidence_level.rationale: Observational studies suggest immunomodulator therapy in SS may prevent lymphoma development. Lancet Rheumatology 2024 multicenter study showed systemic treatment reduces risk of new SS systemic activity. No RCTs specifically addressing lymphoma prevention through SS treatment.

survival_benefit.rationale: No direct lymphoma survival data. Observational data: 0/128 SS patients on immunomodulators developed NHL vs lymphoma in untreated SS patients. Treating underlying SS may reduce ongoing B-cell stimulation driving lymphomagenesis. Reduces relapse risk.

accessibility.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is generic, inexpensive, and widely available. Standard rheumatologic management. No special authorization required. Often already part of Sjogren's management.

safety_profile.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is well-tolerated long-term. Main risk is retinal toxicity with prolonged use (annual eye exams recommended). GI side effects mild. Does not cause significant immunosuppression.

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically relevant to this patient's Sjogren's syndrome which is the driver of MZL development. Addresses the autoimmune root cause of lymphomagenesis. SS activity independently predicts lymphoma with dose-dependent relationship.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Hydroxychloroquine modulates immune function by alkalizing lysosomes, inhibiting antigen processing and presentation, reducing Toll-like receptor signaling (TLR7/9), and decreasing pro-inflammatory cytokine production. In Sjogren's syndrome, this reduces chronic B-cell activation and germinal center-like ectopic lymphoid structures that drive marginal zone lymphomagenesis. Controlling SS disease activity may reduce the ongoing antigenic stimulation of autoreactive B cells.

key_evidence.study_name: Lancet Rheumatology multicenter retrospective study / Taiwan population-based cohort

treatments.name: Epcoritamab (Epkinly) - CD20xCD3 Bispecific Antibody

evidence_level.rationale: FDA-approved for R/R DLBCL. Phase 2 data in indolent NHL including MZL is emerging. Combined with R2, CR rates >80% in FL. Subcutaneous formulation convenient. MZL-specific data limited but extrapolated from indolent NHL trials.

survival_benefit.rationale: High CR rates (>80%) when combined with R2 in indolent NHL. Fixed-duration treatment potential. Durable responses observed. Limited follow-up in MZL specifically. Promising but not yet established for MZL.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for DLBCL only. Not yet approved for MZL. Available through clinical trials. Subcutaneous injection is convenient. Step-up dosing requires initial monitoring period.

safety_profile.rationale: Subcutaneous delivery reduces CRS severity vs IV bispecifics. CRS mostly grade 1-2. Neutropenia manageable. Fixed-duration limits prolonged immunosuppression. Better tolerated than CAR-T.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets CD20 (confirmed positive) and CD3 on T cells. Highly specific for CD20+ B-cell malignancies. Effective regardless of t(11;18) or BCL2 status. May be active in rituximab-refractory cases.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Epcoritamab is a subcutaneously administered CD20xCD3 bispecific antibody that redirects T cells to kill CD20-expressing lymphoma cells. The subcutaneous route of administration provides slower absorption and lower peak concentrations compared to IV bispecifics, potentially reducing CRS severity while maintaining anti-tumor efficacy through sustained T-cell engagement.

key_evidence.study_name: EPCORE NHL trials / Combination studies with R2

treatments.availability: Clinical Trial for MZL / FDA Approved for R/R DLBCL only

treatments.name: Pirtobrutinib (Jaypirca) - Non-Covalent BTK Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1/2 BRUIN trial data in R/R MZL shows ORR 50% (CR 2.8%, PR 47.2%). Median PFS 15.6 months. 36 MZL patients evaluated. Phase 2 study of pirtobrutinib + rituximab in untreated MZL initiated at U of Utah. Not yet approved for MZL.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 50% in heavily pretreated population (median 3 prior lines). Median PFS 15.6 months. Active in patients post-covalent BTK inhibitor failure. Lower response rate than zanubrutinib but effective after BTK inhibitor resistance.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for R/R MCL (not MZL). Available through clinical trials for MZL. Oral once-daily dosing. Phase 2 combination trial with rituximab recruiting for untreated MZL.

safety_profile.rationale: Well tolerated: diarrhea (36.1%), fatigue (33.3%), contusion (30.6%). Grade ≥3 neutropenia 27.8%. Low discontinuation rate (5.6%). Reversible BTK inhibition may allow better recovery. No significant cardiac toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Non-covalent BTK inhibitor effective regardless of BTK C481S mutation status. Active in MZL with BTK-dependent signaling. Overcomes resistance to covalent BTK inhibitors. Does not require specific biomarker beyond MZL diagnosis.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pirtobrutinib is a highly selective, non-covalent (reversible) BTK inhibitor that binds to BTK without forming a covalent bond at C481, enabling it to inhibit both wild-type and C481S-mutant BTK. This reversible binding mechanism overcomes a primary resistance pathway to covalent BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, zanubrutinib) and maintains selective BTK inhibition with minimal off-target effects.
biomarker_requirements: None specific - effective across BTK-dependent B-cell malignancies
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial / Off-label (FDA approved for R/R MCL only)

clinical_trials.title: TRANSCEND FL-MZL: Lisocabtagene Maraleucel (liso-cel) in Relapsed or Refractory Follicular Lymphoma and Marginal Zone Lymphoma
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 84.4% (ITT), CR 55.8%. 24-month PFS 85.7%, OS 90.4%. First CAR-T approved for MZL. Median DOR not reached.

clinical_trials.title: MAGNOLIA: Zanubrutinib in Relapsed/Refractory Marginal Zone Lymphoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Final analysis: ORR 68.2%, CR 25.8%. Nodal MZL ORR 76%. 24-month DOR 72.9%, PFS 70.9%, OS 85.9%. Favorable safety with low AF rate (2.9%).

clinical_trials.title: Pirtobrutinib in Combination with Rituximab in Adults with Untreated Marginal Zone Lymphoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Newly initiated trial. No results yet. Evaluating non-covalent BTK inhibitor + anti-CD20 as first-line chemo-free option for MZL.

clinical_trials.title: Pirtobrutinib in Relapsed/Refractory B-Cell Malignancies Including MZL
clinical_trials.early_results: MZL cohort (n=36): ORR 50%, CR 2.8%. Median PFS 15.6 months. Well-tolerated with 5.6% discontinuation rate. Active post-covalent BTK inhibitor failure.

clinical_trials.title: Epcoritamab in Relapsed/Refractory B-Cell Lymphomas Including Indolent NHL
clinical_trials.early_results: CR rates >80% in FL when combined with R2. MZL-specific data emerging. Subcutaneous formulation with manageable CRS. Phase 3 expansion to indolent NHL subtypes ongoing.

clinical_trials.title: Mosunetuzumab with Response-Driven Lenalidomide Augmentation in Untreated FL and MZL
clinical_trials.early_results: CR rate 88% in MZL, 81% in FL. 12-month PFS 87%. Fixed-duration, chemo-free regimen. Median follow-up 14 months.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combines systemic B-cell depletion with definitive local control for Stage II disease. Rituximab addresses systemic Sjogren's-driven B-cell proliferation and occult distant disease while ISRT provides curative-intent local therapy for bilateral cervical nodes. May reduce out-of-field relapse compared to RT alone.

BR induction achieves deep responses followed by rituximab maintenance to prolong remission duration. For Sjogren's-associated MZL, prolonged B-cell depletion may reduce autoimmune-driven relapse. However, PFS benefit of maintenance in MZL specifically is not established.

Bispecific antibody T-cell engagement combined with lenalidomide immunomodulation and ADCC enhancement. Response-driven approach adds lenalidomide only for patients not achieving CR with mosunetuzumab alone. CR rate 88% in MZL. Fixed-duration, chemo-free combination.

BTK inhibition disrupts BCR signaling while rituximab provides direct CD20-targeted cell killing. Complementary mechanisms may deepen responses beyond either agent alone. Oral + IV combination allows convenient dosing. Synergistic in preclinical models of B-cell lymphomas.

Treating both the lymphoma and the underlying autoimmune driver simultaneously. Lancet Rheumatology 2024 study showed systemic treatment reduced new SS systemic activity by 33%. Addressing the root cause of B-cell activation may reduce lymphoma relapse risk and prevent contralateral or distant recurrence.

supportive_care.evidence: Johns Hopkins Sjogren's Center guidelines; Lancet Rheumatology 2024 multicenter study. SS systemic activity independently predicts lymphoma with dose-dependent relationship.
supportive_care.benefit: Controlling autoimmune B-cell activation reduces lymphomagenesis driver. Immunomodulator use associated with zero NHL development in 128 SS patients vs lymphoma in untreated SS. Reduces risk of new SS systemic activity (33% fewer flares with systemic treatment).

supportive_care.evidence: Sjogren's Syndrome Foundation guidelines. Essential given baseline xerostomia from SS potentially worsened by cervical radiation therapy.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains oral health, reduces dental caries and oral infection risk, improves quality of life. Pilocarpine (5mg TID) and cevimeline (30mg TID) stimulate residual salivary gland function. Critical if radiation therapy is selected.

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN guidelines mandate HBV screening before rituximab. Rituximab can reactivate HBV. Hypogammaglobulinemia monitoring with quantitative immunoglobulins recommended every 3-6 months.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevents potentially fatal HBV reactivation. IgG replacement therapy if levels <400 mg/dL with recurrent infections. Reduces infection-related morbidity and treatment interruptions.

supportive_care.evidence: Transformation to DLBCL occurs in 3-5% of MZL over 10 years. Risk factors include advanced age, elevated LDH, high FLIPI score. PET-CT can detect transformation (high FDG uptake).
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of transformation to aggressive lymphoma enables timely R-CHOP or CAR-T therapy. Regular monitoring with clinical assessment, LDH, and periodic imaging. Biopsy any rapidly growing nodes.

supportive_care.evidence: Meta-analyses in lymphoma survivors show exercise reduces fatigue, improves cardiovascular fitness, and may enhance immune function. Cochrane Review of exercise in hematologic malignancies.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces cancer-related fatigue by 25-30%. Improves quality of life, cardiovascular health, and psychological wellbeing. 150 minutes/week moderate intensity recommended. May reduce Sjogren's fatigue (major symptom).

supportive_care.evidence: Studies show watch-and-wait patients experience significant anxiety. Lymphoma-specific distress screening tools validated. Cognitive behavioral therapy effective for cancer-related anxiety.
supportive_care.benefit: Addresses 'worry and wait' psychological burden, especially relevant if active surveillance chosen. Reduces treatment-related distress and improves adherence. Peer support groups for rare lymphoma subtypes available through Lymphoma Research Foundation.
